speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: The date is December 28th, 1995. We are with Bernard Schuster, B-E-R-N-A-R-D S-C-H-U-S-T-E-R. The interviewer is Deborah Lewin, D-E-B-O-R-A-H L-E-W-I-N. We're in Rochester, New York. And the interview will be conducted in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: I am Deborah Lewin. It is my privilege to be here today with Mr. Bernard Schuster, Bernie, in Rochester, New York. The date is December 28th, 1995.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I am very happy to be here with Deborah Lewin, being interviewed by her. We are old friends. I have been friends with her parents for quite a while. And this is a real privilege.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hee hee.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. OK. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm going to ask you to spell just--

speaker: interviewee
text: I better have something that-- in my hand that'll help me with the spellings.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's just your name and Jasionovka, that's all. During the-- during the actual, we don't have to.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us your name and spell it for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Bernard, B-E-R-N-A-R-D, Schuster, S-C-H-U-S-T-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is the date of your birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born May 17th, 1928.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland, in a small town Jasionovka, J-A-S-H-I-O-N-O-V-K-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what large city in Poland is that near?

speaker: interviewee
text: Near Bialystok, which is B-I-A-L-Y-S-T-O-K.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you now?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am now 67.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were the names of your mother and father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name was Sarah, maiden name Maretzki, M-A-R-E-T-Z-K-I. My father's name was Jacob Schuster. I should also say that in Europe, in Jasionovka, I was known as Berl and not Bernie. And my father was known as Jakov or Yankel, rather than Jacob.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language did you speak at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: At home, we spoke Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you speak-- did you know any other languages?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We-- I knew Hebrew, Polish, Russian, German, and eventually English. I also spoke Italian at one time, but I have forgotten that.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many siblings did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had seven siblings.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Their names were Baile, Feitche, Mariashe, Meyer, Dverke, Tzipe, Maishe, and myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you the youngest?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was the youngest of eight.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you all get along?

speaker: interviewee
text: We got along beautifully, from what I remember. I should say that after 60 years or so, memories begin to fade and I am no longer sure whether I really remember what was, or whether I have embellished, repressed, or modified my memories. But, from what I remember, I had a wonderful childhood.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your family religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Eastern Europe, most Jewish people were Orthodox. There was no Conservative or Reformed denomination at that time. However, within Orthodoxy, there were many, many phases of it. My father was quite liberal and not regarded as a terribly religious person.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sorts of religious observances did you maintain in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: We [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] observed the Shabbat very closely, all the holidays. My father did go to synagogue to pray weekdays, as well, when he could. However, as I will probably have a chance to discuss later, everyone worked so hard that it was very difficult to find time to pray on a daily basis. But certainly holidays and Shabbat was observed very closely.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your family support itself?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father owned a [? tannery ?]. When I was born, he actually worked for someone. And we lived in a very small house in the center of town. Eventually, my father bought a tannery and employed other people. Excuse me. [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe the home that you lived in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Let me take perhaps a segment of time and, in my own words, describe Jasionovka and the home I lived in. Jasionovka was a little town, like thousands and thousands of other towns in Eastern Europe, in the Pale of Settlement. In Yiddish, it was called shtetl. And I think it was the Jewish author Sholem Aleichem who once described-- he said, if you want to know what the map of Eastern Europe looked like and the shtetlach, he says, consider a Friday night challah with poppy seeds sprinkled all over. That's how those towns were sprinkled over Eastern Europe. And Jasionovka was one of those little towns. There were approximately 2,000 people who lived in the town. Most of them were Jewish. There were a few non-Jews. Of course, like all the other towns, we were surrounded by villages which were completely occupied by Polish-- Polish farmers. Jasionovka had no electricity, no paved streets. The homes were not much more than huts, many of them with dirt floors, some of them with wooden floors. We had no running water. Water had to be carried from a well. Wood had to be chopped and brought in, in order to heat and cook with. And life was extremely hard. How in the world by mother managed to raise eight children and keep them all fed and clothed-- to this day I cannot understand it. Somehow, she must have worked 50 hours a day in order to accomplish all of that. The town itself consisted of a major square. There were two squares, actually. There was the center of town, where the Polish church was located, surrounded by a high stone wall which plays a part in my story. And our school, the Hebrew school, was located in that square. Some of the shops, bakery shops, grocery stores, such as they were, were located in that square. And then there were some houses. People lived in various parts of the square. Then there was a second square backing up to the large square, where there were at least two and probably several small additional synagogues. There was the big synagogue, which was a stone structure. There was a wooden synagogue next to it. And then there were a number of homes where there were small temples where people would pray. They would divide up based on trades. For instance, shoemakers would have their own praying shtiebel they called it. Tailors would have their own shtiebel. And so life really revolved to a large extent around this square and around the religious aspects of town. We also had a small library. And I'll never forget my brother Meyer, who was the third of the oldest siblings, after Baile and Feitche, used to carry me on his shoulders-- I remember that very clearly-- to the library, where he would read books to me when I was too little to read myself, where eventually he introduced me to books. I still remember a book he read to me that was seven or eight volumes, called [SPEAKING YIDDISH], the Don River. And I remember him reading it to me. I also remember teaching me chess, how to play chess. And to this day I enjoy chess because of my brother Meyer. Oh, it was a wonderful thing to be a child in Jasionovka, the youngest of eight children. By the time I was born, my oldest sister was ready to get married. In fact, my niece Roske, who now lives in Australia, is only three years younger than myself, so I became an uncle at age three. And, of course, all of my older brothers and sisters, who had boyfriends and then husbands and wives, they thought I was a toy, so they played with me. They spoiled me rotten. I was probably the most spoiled child you have ever seen in your life, with eight brothers-- you know seven brothers and sisters and their boyfriends and spouses just believing that I could be played with like a toy. It was a very wonderful childhood. And I think the security and the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] joy that I took with me from my childhood is what really managed to get me through the Holocaust and the difficult years that followed. I believe if I had not had that strength and that security, I probably would not have survived, because I wouldn't have had the-- what it took to go through the horrible things that followed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that briefly gives you a picture of life in Jasionovka. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I can go on and tell you that when I was born, we lived on a little street that was nothing but a dirt street with a few houses on it, at the end of which there was a windmill, on a hill. And I remember having so much fun climbing on that hill and playing near the windmill and playing everywhere.

speaker: interviewee
text: In back of us-- we lived in a very small house. We had a garden. And at the end of the garden was an outhouse. There was no running water, so there were-- was no inside plumbing. And so we had to grow our own vegetables. And at the end of the garden was an outhouse. And in back of that was a large orchard. And I remember my brother Maishe, who was four years older than I, and I, as a youngster, stealing fruit from that neighbor's orchard, because we said, well, the threes are leaning over our fence, so we are justified in doing so. Oh, it was so much fun. It was really a wonderful childhood.

speaker: interviewer
text: How large would you estimate your extended family to be?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, look at it this way. My father was one of eight children. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In my family, there were eight. In all of my father's siblings, there were 7, 8, 9, 10 children. Therefore, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] just uncles and aunts, I probably had close to 100. And many of them-- most of them lived in or near Jasionovka. Now, if you go down one generation, to first cousins, you are talking about probably 200 first cousins. To this day, even after the Holocaust, some 15 years ago when we visited Israel, one of my second cousins brought together something like 30 either first, second, or third cousins to meet us. So we-- we were a very large family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe Shabbat at your house.

speaker: interviewee
text: It has been said that rather than the-- the Jewish people keeping the Shabbat, the Shabbat had kept the Jewish people. And I think that there is a great deal of truth to it. Life in the shtetl was terribly difficult. Aside from the anti-Semitism and the isolation, which I will talk about, I'm sure, later, people worked day and night. It was very difficult to work in a tannery and support a family of eight children. In fact, it was more than just eight children, because in most families grandparents lived in the house with you. And aunts and uncles who never got married lived in the house. So there were many mouths to feed and life was very difficult. And people worked 12-, 14-, 15-hour days. But, came Friday afternoon, life changed. A quiet befell the town. Men would stop working. They would go to the bathhouse, to shower and clean and get ready for the Shabbat. Then they would come home, take their children, and walk to the synagogue. My father never came home from the synagogue without bringing at least one, and sometimes two or three, guests for the Shabbat. Those were called orchim, people-- they were religious students or traveling scholars who did not have a place. There were no hotels, so many of them ended up coming as guests for the entire weekend. There were also, in some towns, and occasionally they would come to Jasionovka, there were yeshivot. Those are religious schools for young men. And they would have no way of existing, except by being supported by the townspeople. In fact, it was called in Yiddish essen teg, which means 'to eat days.' And what it meant was that Yosel the student would be assigned Monday he would eat at Yankel's, and Tuesday he would eat at Schlaime's, and Wednesday he would eat at [INAUDIBLE], and so on and so forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was called essen teg. There were established days when this yeshiva student would eat with a certain family. And on the Shabbat we always had somebody at our house.

speaker: interviewee
text: They served a function. Aside from giving people a chance to be charitable, they also brought news from the outside world. Remember, we had no radio and no newspapers and certainly no television. And the news had to travel through-- from mouth to mouth. And those traveling scholars or travelling people who would end up in Jasionovka for the weekend, for the Shabbat, would bring news from the outside world as to what's going on. They would also-- if they were scholars, they would test the children to see how much they know and how much they've learned. There was always a testing process going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So describe your schooling.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as I earlier said, my father was not terribly religious by European standards. Therefore, by the time I came around, there were many new movements and new winds were blowing through Eastern Europe. Rather than being totally preoccupied with studying of the Torah and being religious and devoting your entire life to it, people begin to think about Zionism. In fact, some of my sisters went to Zionist schools. There was also a socialist movement, the Bund.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of school did you go to?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to a Hebrew school called Beit Sefer Tarbut, which is a tarbut school. However, I also studied Yiddish there and Polish and German, in that same school. It was a very small school. It was in the center of town, on a hill. Right next to us were-- was a hat-maker. He was actually connected to this school. His house was part of the school building. And I remember we used to hide in his shop and watch how hats are being made. It was a one-story building. And on summer days, the windows would be open. I remember many times playing tricks on the teachers and then jumping through the windows and hiding outside of the school. We had a lot of fun.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a favorite topic, a favorite subject?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I always liked geography, and I always liked to draw. And I'd like to tell you a story about my drawing, my sketching. I think I must have been in maybe third or fourth grade, when our teacher was a beautiful young woman. And I fell madly in love with her. I adored that teacher.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I cannot remember. But rather than listening to her-- she would be in front of the class, and I would be sitting there and instead of taking notes, I would be drawing sketches of her. I had a whole notebook of sketches of that teacher. And one day I was preoccupied with my drawing, when she walked around and came to the back of the room and walked behind me. And, without my knowing it, she saw what I was doing. And at the end of the class, she asked me to stay behind.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] everyone left, she said, may I see your sketchbook? And I showed it to her. And she took me in her arms and gave me a kiss, which I'll never forget. And then she said, would you mind if I borrowed this sketchbook? I'd like to have it for a while. And I said no, I wouldn't.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she kept it for quite a while. And at the end of the summer, one day she called me-- she lived in a house. She was renting a room just half a block away from the school. She called me to her house and said, I'd like you to have the notebook back. And again she gave me a hug and a kiss. And that afternoon she went to the cemetery and she committed suicide. And the reason she committed suicide is that she was in love with a young man, who was the first man in town to have a motorcycle. He was the director of a large tannery, Minsky Tanneries, that was in town. That was the major industry in the town. And evidently the love affair went sour and she killed herself by drinking iodine. And I have never forgotten that. I was heartbroken for years to come, because the teacher I loved killed herself.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your goals, as a young person looking towards the future?

speaker: interviewee
text: Until 1941, I really don't know what my goals were. I was just a carefree, spoiled child, trying to have as much fun as possible and learn as little as possible. And that was just about it. And to stay alive. I haven't discussed yet the outside world there, and the insecurity of the outside. I've only talked about the security within the home. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Everything changed in 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before 1939, how would you describe the relationships between Jews and non-Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I would say that it was probably-- within the town, the Jews had a secure and intimate existence. For instance, we had no police force. We didn't need one. We had almost no divorces. There was some spouse abusal. Husbands were beating wives occasionally, so I have heard, but very little of it. We had no delinquency, no divorce, no-- not many suicides. Life was very calm. Most people were born, lived all their lives, and died in the same town. In fact, I have an article, here, on my desk in Yiddish that shows that Jews settled in Jasionovka in 1731. And I have no reason to doubt that some of my own family were the first settlers, because whenever I would ask my father or my grandfather, Grandpa, where did you come from, he would say, Jasionovka. And so, since my father was born sometimes in the 19th century, and my grandfather probably early part of the 19th century, I have no reason to think that they were not settlers in Jasionovka as early as 1731.

speaker: interviewer
text: As a young person, did you experience anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Now, The best way to describe the existence of Jewish people in the small towns was it was like a-- an island of serenity in a sea of hostility. Although the town itself was serene and in many ways a happy place to grow up and to live, poverty and hard work notwithstanding, we were surrounded by Poles who refused to let us become part of their society-- part of their surroundings. Therefore, many Jewish people in the town never even learned to speak Polish, because there was so little interaction between the Poles and the Jews. There was only a few occasions when Poles and Jews would be together. One of them, which occurred several times a year, was a market day. In Yiddish it was called yarid. In fact, that was a very major event in the life of the shtetl, because in the shtetl there were many shoemakers, tailors, leather producers, candle-makers, and so on and so forth. They would produce things that the farmers and the Poles needed.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you experience that day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'll tell you in a moment. We had the things that the farmers needed and the farmers had the food, the chickens, the wheat, the flour, the vegetables, that we needed. So there would be market days when all the farmers would come to town and they would set up their wagons in a circle, all around that large square that I mentioned, near the shops and near the church and so on. And the Jews would also set up shop. And then there would be bartering, very little money exchanged, just one chicken for a pair of shoes or whatever. And this is how it went on. And normally it would be a happy day. But every now and then, the farmers would start drinking. And before you know it, one would say, well, let's have a little fun. And then they would say, well, let's beat up a few Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: So suddenly they would start a pogrom, which was simply a disorganized, violent outrage, where women would be raped, houses would be burned, people would be killed, and it would be total chaos for the rest of the day, until things would quiet down, lives would be rebuilt again, and started all over again.
